message.content:
Define a specification-driven guild operations system that unifies economic planning and raid readiness validation by binding real-time market data to formal raid preparation contracts. The system ingests auction house data and character profiles (augmented with guild metadata), models consumables, materials, and role-specific requirements, and continuously computes both guild-level deficits and player-level readiness against a defined specification. It generates deterministic outputs including economic recommendations (BUY, FARM, WAIT) based on price trends and demand, and readiness classifications (READY, WARNING, NOT_READY) with explicit violations and remediation guidance.

The novel step is the integration of a spec-native control plane that treats both resource acquisition (gold/materials) and player preparation as machine-checkable invariants, enabling continuous validation and decision-making across the full raid value stream. By linking market conditions directly to readiness requirements, the system shifts guild coordination from reactive and manual processes to a unified, automated, and outcome-aligned workflow, with optional drift detection to identify when economic state or player readiness falls out of compliance prior to raid execution. my API key is Credentials

# Feature Specification: [FEATURE NAME]

**Feature Branch**: `[###-feature-name]`  
**Created**: [DATE]  
**Status**: Draft  
**Input**: User description: "$ARGUMENTS"

## User Scenarios & Testing *(mandatory)*

<!--
  IMPORTANT: User stories should be PRIORITIZED as user journeys ordered by importance.
  Each user story/journey must be INDEPENDENTLY TESTABLE - meaning if you implement just ONE of them,
  you should still have a viable MVP (Minimum Viable Product) that delivers value.
  
  Assign priorities (P1, P2, P3, etc.) to each story, where P1 is the most critical.
  Think of each story as a standalone slice of functionality that can be:
  - Developed independently
  - Tested independently
  - Deployed independently
  - Demonstrated to users independently
-->

### User Story 1 - [Brief Title] (Priority: P1)

[Describe this user journey in plain language]

**Why this priority**: [Explain the value and why it has this priority level]

**Independent Test**: [Describe how this can be tested independently - e.g., "Can be fully tested by [specific action] and delivers [specific value]"]

**Acceptance Scenarios**:

1. **Given** [initial state], **When** [action], **Then** [expected outcome]
2. **Given** [initial state], **When** [action], **Then** [expected outcome]

---

### User Story 2 - [Brief Title] (Priority: P2)

[Describe this user journey in plain language]

**Why this priority**: [Explain the value and why it has this priority level]

**Independent Test**: [Describe how this can be tested independently]

**Acceptance Scenarios**:

1. **Given** [initial state], **When** [action], **Then** [expected outcome]

---

### User Story 3 - [Brief Title] (Priority: P3)

[Describe this user journey in plain language]

**Why this priority**: [Explain the value and why it has this priority level]

**Independent Test**: [Describe how this can be tested independently]

**Acceptance Scenarios**:

1. **Given** [initial state], **When** [action], **Then** [expected outcome]

---

[Add more user stories as needed, each with an assigned priority]

### Edge Cases

<!--
  ACTION REQUIRED: The content in this section represents placeholders.
  Fill them out with the right edge cases.
-->

- What happens when [boundary condition]?
- How does system handle [error scenario]?

## Requirements *(mandatory)*

<!--
  ACTION REQUIRED: The content in this section represents placeholders.
  Fill them out with the right functional requirements.
-->

### Functional Requirements

- **FR-001**: System MUST [specific capability, e.g., "allow users to create accounts"]
- **FR-002**: System MUST [specific capability, e.g., "validate email addresses"]  
- **FR-003**: Users MUST be able to [key interaction, e.g., "reset their password"]
- **FR-004**: System MUST [data requirement, e.g., "persist user preferences"]
- **FR-005**: System MUST [behavior, e.g., "log all security events"]

*Example of marking unclear requirements:*

- **FR-006**: System MUST authenticate users via [NEEDS CLARIFICATION: auth method not specified - email/password, SSO, OAuth?]
- **FR-007**: System MUST retain user data for [NEEDS CLARIFICATION: retention period not specified]

### Key Entities *(include if feature involves data)*

- **[Entity 1]**: [What it represents, key attributes without implementation]
- **[Entity 2]**: [What it represents, relationships to other entities]

## Success Criteria *(mandatory)*

<!--
  ACTION REQUIRED: Define measurable success criteria.
  These must be technology-agnostic and measurable.
-->

### Measurable Outcomes

- **SC-001**: [Measurable metric, e.g., "Users can complete account creation in under 2 minutes"]
- **SC-002**: [Measurable metric, e.g., "System handles 1000 concurrent users without degradation"]
- **SC-003**: [User satisfaction metric, e.g., "90% of users successfully complete primary task on first attempt"]
- **SC-004**: [Business metric, e.g., "Reduce support tickets related to [X] by 50%"]

## Assumptions

<!--
  ACTION REQUIRED: The content in this section represents placeholders.
  Fill them out with the right assumptions based on reasonable defaults
  chosen when the feature description did not specify certain details.
-->

- [Assumption about target users, e.g., "Users have stable internet connectivity"]
- [Assumption about scope boundaries, e.g., "Mobile support is out of scope for v1"]
- [Assumption about data/environment, e.g., "Existing authentication system will be reused"]
- [Dependency on existing system/service, e.g., "Requires access to the existing user profile API"]

# [PROJECT_NAME] Constitution
<!-- Example: Spec Constitution, TaskFlow Constitution, etc. -->

## Core Principles

### [PRINCIPLE_1_NAME]
<!-- Example: I. Library-First -->
[PRINCIPLE_1_DESCRIPTION]
<!-- Example: Every feature starts as a standalone library; Libraries must be self-contained, independently testable, documented; Clear purpose required - no organizational-only libraries -->

### [PRINCIPLE_2_NAME]
<!-- Example: II. CLI Interface -->
[PRINCIPLE_2_DESCRIPTION]
<!-- Example: Every library exposes functionality via CLI; Text in/out protocol: stdin/args → stdout, errors → stderr; Support JSON + human-readable formats -->

### [PRINCIPLE_3_NAME]
<!-- Example: III. Test-First (NON-NEGOTIABLE) -->
[PRINCIPLE_3_DESCRIPTION]
<!-- Example: TDD mandatory: Tests written → User approved → Tests fail → Then implement; Red-Green-Refactor cycle strictly enforced -->

### [PRINCIPLE_4_NAME]
<!-- Example: IV. Integration Testing -->
[PRINCIPLE_4_DESCRIPTION]
<!-- Example: Focus areas requiring integration tests: New library contract tests, Contract changes, Inter-service communication, Shared schemas -->

### [PRINCIPLE_5_NAME]
<!-- Example: V. Observability, VI. Versioning & Breaking Changes, VII. Simplicity -->
[PRINCIPLE_5_DESCRIPTION]
<!-- Example: Text I/O ensures debuggability; Structured logging required; Or: MAJOR.MINOR.BUILD format; Or: Start simple, YAGNI principles -->

## [SECTION_2_NAME]
<!-- Example: Additional Constraints, Security Requirements, Performance Standards, etc. -->

[SECTION_2_CONTENT]
<!-- Example: Technology stack requirements, compliance standards, deployment policies, etc. -->

## [SECTION_3_NAME]
<!-- Example: Development Workflow, Review Process, Quality Gates, etc. -->

[SECTION_3_CONTENT]
<!-- Example: Code review requirements, testing gates, deployment approval process, etc. -->

## Governance
<!-- Example: Constitution supersedes all other practices; Amendments require documentation, approval, migration plan -->

[GOVERNANCE_RULES]
<!-- Example: All PRs/reviews must verify compliance; Complexity must be justified; Use [GUIDANCE_FILE] for runtime development guidance -->

**Version**: [CONSTITUTION_VERSION] | **Ratified**: [RATIFICATION_DATE] | **Last Amended**: [LAST_AMENDED_DATE]
<!-- Example: Version: 2.1.1 | Ratified: 2025-06-13 | Last Amended: 2025-07-16 -->

content.content:
1	---
2	name: "speckit-specify"
3	description: "Create or update the feature specification from a natural language feature description."
4	argument-hint: "Describe the feature you want to specify"
5	compatibility: "Requires spec-kit project structure with .specify/ directory"
6	metadata:
7	  author: "github-spec-kit"
8	  source: "templates/commands/specify.md"
9	user-invocable: true
10	disable-model-invocation: true
11	---
12	
13	
14	## User Input
15	
16	```text
17	$ARGUMENTS
18	```
19	
20	You **MUST** consider the user input before proceeding (if not empty).
21	
22	## Pre-Execution Checks
23	
24	**Check for extension hooks (before specification)**:
25	- Check if `.specify/extensions.yml` exists in the project root.
26	- If it exists, read it and look for entries under the `hooks.before_specify` key
27	- If the YAML cannot be parsed or is invalid, skip hook checking silently and continue normally
28	- Filter out hooks where `enabled` is explicitly `false`. Treat hooks without an `enabled` field as enabled by default.
29	- For each remaining hook, do **not** attempt to interpret or evaluate hook `condition` expressions:
30	  - If the hook has no `condition` field, or it is null/empty, treat the hook as executable
31	  - If the hook defines a non-empty `condition`, skip the hook and leave condition evaluation to the HookExecutor implementation
32	- For each executable hook, output the following based on its `optional` flag:
33	  - **Optional hook** (`optional: true`):
34	    ```
35	    ## Extension Hooks
36	
37	    **Optional Pre-Hook**: {extension}
38	    Command: `/{command}`
39	    Description: {description}
40	
41	    Prompt: {prompt}
42	    To execute: `/{command}`
43	    ```
44	  - **Mandatory hook** (`optional: false`):
45	    ```
46	    ## Extension Hooks
47	
48	    **Automatic Pre-Hook**: {extension}
49	    Executing: `/{command}`
50	    EXECUTE_COMMAND: {command}
51	
52	    Wait for the result of the hook command before proceeding to the Outline.
53	    ```
54	- If no hooks are registered or `.specify/extensions.yml` does not exist, skip silently
55	
56	## Outline
57	
58	The text the user typed after `/speckit.specify` in the triggering message **is** the feature description. Assume you always have it available in this conversation even if `$ARGUMENTS` appears literally below. Do not ask the user to repeat it unless they provided an empty command.
59	
60	Given that feature description, do this:
61	
62	1. **Generate a concise short name** (2-4 words) for the branch:
63	   - Analyze the feature description and extract the most meaningful keywords
64	   - Create a 2-4 word short name that captures the essence of the feature
65	   - Use action-noun format when possible (e.g., "add-user-auth", "fix-payment-bug")
66	   - Preserve technical terms and acronyms (OAuth2, API, JWT, etc.)
67	   - Keep it concise but descriptive enough to understand the feature at a glance
68	   - Examples:
69	     - "I want to add user authentication" → "user-auth"
70	     - "Implement OAuth2 integration for the API" → "oauth2-api-integration"
71	     - "Create a dashboard for analytics" → "analytics-dashboard"
72	     - "Fix payment processing timeout bug" → "fix-payment-timeout"
73	
74	2. **Create the feature branch** by running the script with `--short-name` (and `--json`). In sequential mode, do NOT pass `--number` — the script auto-detects the next available number. In timestamp mode, the script generates a `YYYYMMDD-HHMMSS` prefix automatically:
75	
76	   **Branch numbering mode**: Before running the script, check if `.specify/init-options.json` exists and read the `branch_numbering` value.
77	   - If `"timestamp"`, add `--timestamp` (Bash) or `-Timestamp` (PowerShell) to the script invocation
78	   - If `"sequential"` or absent, do not add any extra flag (default behavior)
79	
80	   - Bash example: `.specify/scripts/powershell/create-new-feature.ps1 "$ARGUMENTS" --json --short-name "user-auth" "Add user authentication"`
81	   - Bash (timestamp): `.specify/scripts/powershell/create-new-feature.ps1 "$ARGUMENTS" --json --timestamp --short-name "user-auth" "Add user authentication"`
82	   - PowerShell example: `.specify/scripts/powershell/create-new-feature.ps1 "$ARGUMENTS" -Json -ShortName "user-auth" "Add user authentication"`
83	   - PowerShell (timestamp): `.specify/scripts/powershell/create-new-feature.ps1 "$ARGUMENTS" -Json -Timestamp -ShortName "user-auth" "Add user authentication"`
84	
85	   **IMPORTANT**:
86	   - Do NOT pass `--number` — the script determines the correct next number automatically
87	   - Always include the JSON flag (`--json` for Bash, `-Json` for PowerShell) so the output can be parsed reliably
88	   - You must only ever run this script once per feature
89	   - The JSON is provided in the terminal as output - always refer to it to get the actual content you're looking for
90	   - The JSON output will contain BRANCH_NAME and SPEC_FILE paths
91	   - For single quotes in args like "I'm Groot", use escape syntax: e.g 'I'\''m Groot' (or double-quote if possible: "I'm Groot")
92	
93	3. Load `.specify/templates/spec-template.md` to understand required sections.
94	
95	4. Follow this execution flow:
96	
97	    1. Parse user description from Input
98	       If empty: ERROR "No feature description provided"
99	    2. Extract key concepts from description
100	       Identify: actors, actions, data, constraints
101	    3. For unclear aspects:
102	       - Make informed guesses based on context and industry standards
103	       - Only mark with [NEEDS CLARIFICATION: specific question] if:
104	         - The choice significantly impacts feature scope or user experience
105	         - Multiple reasonable interpretations exist with different implications
106	         - No reasonable default exists
107	       - **LIMIT: Maximum 3 [NEEDS CLARIFICATION] markers total**
108	       - Prioritize clarifications by impact: scope > security/privacy > user experience > technical details
109	    4. Fill User Scenarios & Testing section
110	       If no clear user flow: ERROR "Cannot determine user scenarios"
111	    5. Generate Functional Requirements
112	       Each requirement must be testable
113	       Use reasonable defaults for unspecified details (document assumptions in Assumptions section)
114	    6. Define Success Criteria
115	       Create measurable, technology-agnostic outcomes
116	       Include both quantitative metrics (time, performance, volume) and qualitative measures (user satisfaction, task completion)
117	       Each criterion must be verifiable without implementation details
118	    7. Identify Key Entities (if data involved)
119	    8. Return: SUCCESS (spec ready for planning)
120	
121	5. Write the specification to SPEC_FILE using the template structure, replacing placeholders with concrete details derived from the feature description (arguments) while preserving section order and headings.
122	
123	6. **Specification Quality Validation**: After writing the initial spec, validate it against quality criteria:
124	
125	   a. **Create Spec Quality Checklist**: Generate a checklist file at `FEATURE_DIR/checklists/requirements.md` using the checklist template structure with these validation items:
126	
127	      ```markdown
128	      # Specification Quality Checklist: [FEATURE NAME]
129	      
130	      **Purpose**: Validate specification completeness and quality before proceeding to planning
131	      **Created**: [DATE]
132	      **Feature**: [Link to spec.md]
133	      
134	      ## Content Quality
135	      
136	      - [ ] No implementation details (languages, frameworks, APIs)
137	      - [ ] Focused on user value and business needs
138	      - [ ] Written for non-technical stakeholders
139	      - [ ] All mandatory sections completed
140	      
141	      ## Requirement Completeness
142	      
143	      - [ ] No [NEEDS CLARIFICATION] markers remain
144	      - [ ] Requirements are testable and unambiguous
145	      - [ ] Success criteria are measurable
146	      - [ ] Success criteria are technology-agnostic (no implementation details)
147	      - [ ] All acceptance scenarios are defined
148	      - [ ] Edge cases are identified
149	      - [ ] Scope is clearly bounded
150	      - [ ] Dependencies and assumptions identified
151	      
152	      ## Feature Readiness
153	      
154	      - [ ] All functional requirements have clear acceptance criteria
155	      - [ ] User scenarios cover primary flows
156	      - [ ] Feature meets measurable outcomes defined in Success Criteria
157	      - [ ] No implementation details leak into specification
158	      
159	      ## Notes
160	      
161	      - Items marked incomplete require spec updates before `/speckit.clarify` or `/speckit.plan`
162	      ```
163	
164	   b. **Run Validation Check**: Review the spec against each checklist item:
165	      - For each item, determine if it passes or fails
166	      - Document specific issues found (quote relevant spec sections)
167	
168	   c. **Handle Validation Results**:
169	
170	      - **If all items pass**: Mark checklist complete and proceed to step 7
171	
172	      - **If items fail (excluding [NEEDS CLARIFICATION])**:
173	        1. List the failing items and specific issues
174	        2. Update the spec to address each issue
175	        3. Re-run validation until all items pass (max 3 iterations)
176	        4. If still failing after 3 iterations, document remaining issues in checklist notes and warn user
177	
178	      - **If [NEEDS CLARIFICATION] markers remain**:
179	        1. Extract all [NEEDS CLARIFICATION: ...] markers from the spec
180	        2. **LIMIT CHECK**: If more than 3 markers exist, keep only the 3 most critical (by scope/security/UX impact) and make informed guesses for the rest
181	        3. For each clarification needed (max 3), present options to user in this format:
182	
183	           ```markdown
184	           ## Question [N]: [Topic]
185	           
186	           **Context**: [Quote relevant spec section]
187	           
188	           **What we need to know**: [Specific question from NEEDS CLARIFICATION marker]
189	           
190	           **Suggested Answers**:
191	           
192	           | Option | Answer | Implications |
193	           |--------|--------|--------------|
194	           | A      | [First suggested answer] | [What this means for the feature] |
195	           | B      | [Second suggested answer] | [What this means for the feature] |
196	           | C      | [Third suggested answer] | [What this means for the feature] |
197	           | Custom | Provide your own answer | [Explain how to provide custom input] |
198	           
199	           **Your choice**: _[Wait for user response]_
200	           ```
201	
202	        4. **CRITICAL - Table Formatting**: Ensure markdown tables are properly formatted:
203	           - Use consistent spacing with pipes aligned
204	           - Each cell should have spaces around content: `| Content |` not `|Content|`
205	           - Header separator must have at least 3 dashes: `|--------|`
206	           - Test that the table renders correctly in markdown preview
207	        5. Number questions sequentially (Q1, Q2, Q3 - max 3 total)
208	        6. Present all questions together before waiting for responses
209	        7. Wait for user to respond with their choices for all questions (e.g., "Q1: A, Q2: Custom - [details], Q3: B")
210	        8. Update the spec by replacing each [NEEDS CLARIFICATION] marker with the user's selected or provided answer
211	        9. Re-run validation after all clarifications are resolved
212	
213	   d. **Update Checklist**: After each validation iteration, update the checklist file with current pass/fail status
214	
215	7. Report completion with branch name, spec file path, checklist results, and readiness for the next phase (`/speckit.clarify` or `/speckit.plan`).
216	
217	8. **Check for extension hooks**: After reporting completion, check if `.specify/extensions.yml` exists in the project root.
218	   - If it exists, read it and look for entries under the `hooks.after_specify` key
219	   - If the YAML cannot be parsed or is invalid, skip hook checking silently and continue normally
220	   - Filter out hooks where `enabled` is explicitly `false`. Treat hooks without an `enabled` field as enabled by default.
221	   - For each remaining hook, do **not** attempt to interpret or evaluate hook `condition` expressions:
222	     - If the hook has no `condition` field, or it is null/empty, treat the hook as executable
223	     - If the hook defines a non-empty `condition`, skip the hook and leave condition evaluation to the HookExecutor implementation
224	   - For each executable hook, output the following based on its `optional` flag:
225	     - **Optional hook** (`optional: true`):
226	       ```
227	       ## Extension Hooks
228	
229	       **Optional Hook**: {extension}
230	       Command: `/{command}`
231	       Description: {description}
232	
233	       Prompt: {prompt}
234	       To execute: `/{command}`
235	       ```
236	     - **Mandatory hook** (`optional: false`):
237	       ```
238	       ## Extension Hooks
239	
240	       **Automatic Hook**: {extension}
241	       Executing: `/{command}`
242	       EXECUTE_COMMAND: {command}
243	       ```
244	   - If no hooks are registered or `.specify/extensions.yml` does not exist, skip silently
245	
246	**NOTE:** The script creates and checks out the new branch and initializes the spec file before writing.
247	
248	## Quick Guidelines
249	
250	- Focus on **WHAT** users need and **WHY**.
251	- Avoid HOW to implement (no tech stack, APIs, code structure).
252	- Written for business stakeholders, not developers.
253	- DO NOT create any checklists that are embedded in the spec. That will be a separate command.
254	
255	### Section Requirements
256	
257	- **Mandatory sections**: Must be completed for every feature
258	- **Optional sections**: Include only when relevant to the feature
259	- When a section doesn't apply, remove it entirely (don't leave as "N/A")
260	
261	### For AI Generation
262	
263	When creating this spec from a user prompt:
264	
265	1. **Make informed guesses**: Use context, industry standards, and common patterns to fill gaps
266	2. **Document assumptions**: Record reasonable defaults in the Assumptions section
267	3. **Limit clarifications**: Maximum 3 [NEEDS CLARIFICATION] markers - use only for critical decisions that:
268	   - Significantly impact feature scope or user experience
269	   - Have multiple reasonable interpretations with different implications
270	   - Lack any reasonable default
271	4. **Prioritize clarifications**: scope > security/privacy > user experience > technical details
272	5. **Think like a tester**: Every vague requirement should fail the "testable and unambiguous" checklist item
273	6. **Common areas needing clarification** (only if no reasonable default exists):
274	   - Feature scope and boundaries (include/exclude specific use cases)
275	   - User types and permissions (if multiple conflicting interpretations possible)
276	   - Security/compliance requirements (when legally/financially significant)
277	
278	**Examples of reasonable defaults** (don't ask about these):
279	
280	- Data retention: Industry-standard practices for the domain
281	- Performance targets: Standard web/mobile app expectations unless specified
282	- Error handling: User-friendly messages with appropriate fallbacks
283	- Authentication method: Standard session-based or OAuth2 for web apps
284	- Integration patterns: Use project-appropriate patterns (REST/GraphQL for web services, function calls for libraries, CLI args for tools, etc.)
285	
286	### Success Criteria Guidelines
287	
288	Success criteria must be:
289	
290	1. **Measurable**: Include specific metrics (time, percentage, count, rate)
291	2. **Technology-agnostic**: No mention of frameworks, languages, databases, or tools
292	3. **User-focused**: Describe outcomes from user/business perspective, not system internals
293	4. **Verifiable**: Can be tested/validated without knowing implementation details
294	
295	**Good examples**:
296	
297	- "Users can complete checkout in under 3 minutes"
298	- "System supports 10,000 concurrent users"
299	- "95% of searches return results in under 1 second"
300	- "Task completion rate improves by 40%"
301	
302	**Bad examples** (implementation-focused):
303	
304	- "API response time is under 200ms" (too technical, use "Users see results instantly")
305	- "Database can handle 1000 TPS" (implementation detail, use user-facing metric)
306	- "React components render efficiently" (framework-specific)
307	- "Redis cache hit rate above 80%" (technology-specific)
308

file.content:
---
name: "speckit-specify"
description: "Create or update the feature specification from a natural language feature description."
argument-hint: "Describe the feature you want to specify"
compatibility: "Requires spec-kit project structure with .specify/ directory"
metadata:
  author: "github-spec-kit"
  source: "templates/commands/specify.md"
user-invocable: true
disable-model-invocation: true
---


## User Input

```text
$ARGUMENTS
```

You **MUST** consider the user input before proceeding (if not empty).

## Pre-Execution Checks

**Check for extension hooks (before specification)**:
- Check if `.specify/extensions.yml` exists in the project root.
- If it exists, read it and look for entries under the `hooks.before_specify` key
- If the YAML cannot be parsed or is invalid, skip hook checking silently and continue normally
- Filter out hooks where `enabled` is explicitly `false`. Treat hooks without an `enabled` field as enabled by default.
- For each remaining hook, do **not** attempt to interpret or evaluate hook `condition` expressions:
  - If the hook has no `condition` field, or it is null/empty, treat the hook as executable
  - If the hook defines a non-empty `condition`, skip the hook and leave condition evaluation to the HookExecutor implementation
- For each executable hook, output the following based on its `optional` flag:
  - **Optional hook** (`optional: true`):
    ```
    ## Extension Hooks

    **Optional Pre-Hook**: {extension}
    Command: `/{command}`
    Description: {description}

    Prompt: {prompt}
    To execute: `/{command}`
    ```
  - **Mandatory hook** (`optional: false`):
    ```
    ## Extension Hooks

    **Automatic Pre-Hook**: {extension}
    Executing: `/{command}`
    EXECUTE_COMMAND: {command}

    Wait for the result of the hook command before proceeding to the Outline.
    ```
- If no hooks are registered or `.specify/extensions.yml` does not exist, skip silently

## Outline

The text the user typed after `/speckit.specify` in the triggering message **is** the feature description. Assume you always have it available in this conversation even if `$ARGUMENTS` appears literally below. Do not ask the user to repeat it unless they provided an empty command.

Given that feature description, do this:

1. **Generate a concise short name** (2-4 words) for the branch:
   - Analyze the feature description and extract the most meaningful keywords
   - Create a 2-4 word short name that captures the essence of the feature
   - Use action-noun format when possible (e.g., "add-user-auth", "fix-payment-bug")
   - Preserve technical terms and acronyms (OAuth2, API, JWT, etc.)
   - Keep it concise but descriptive enough to understand the feature at a glance
   - Examples:
     - "I want to add user authentication" → "user-auth"
     - "Implement OAuth2 integration for the API" → "oauth2-api-integration"
     - "Create a dashboard for analytics" → "analytics-dashboard"
     - "Fix payment processing timeout bug" → "fix-payment-timeout"

2. **Create the feature branch** by running the script with `--short-name` (and `--json`). In sequential mode, do NOT pass `--number` — the script auto-detects the next available number. In timestamp mode, the script generates a `YYYYMMDD-HHMMSS` prefix automatically:

   **Branch numbering mode**: Before running the script, check if `.specify/init-options.json` exists and read the `branch_numbering` value.
   - If `"timestamp"`, add `--timestamp` (Bash) or `-Timestamp` (PowerShell) to the script invocation
   - If `"sequential"` or absent, do not add any extra flag (default behavior)

   - Bash example: `.specify/scripts/powershell/create-new-feature.ps1 "$ARGUMENTS" --json --short-name "user-auth" "Add user authentication"`
   - Bash (timestamp): `.specify/scripts/powershell/create-new-feature.ps1 "$ARGUMENTS" --json --timestamp --short-name "user-auth" "Add user authentication"`
   - PowerShell example: `.specify/scripts/powershell/create-new-feature.ps1 "$ARGUMENTS" -Json -ShortName "user-auth" "Add user authentication"`
   - PowerShell (timestamp): `.specify/scripts/powershell/create-new-feature.ps1 "$ARGUMENTS" -Json -Timestamp -ShortName "user-auth" "Add user authentication"`

   **IMPORTANT**:
   - Do NOT pass `--number` — the script determines the correct next number automatically
   - Always include the JSON flag (`--json` for Bash, `-Json` for PowerShell) so the output can be parsed reliably
   - You must only ever run this script once per feature
   - The JSON is provided in the terminal as output - always refer to it to get the actual content you're looking for
   - The JSON output will contain BRANCH_NAME and SPEC_FILE paths
   - For single quotes in args like "I'm Groot", use escape syntax: e.g 'I'\''m Groot' (or double-quote if possible: "I'm Groot")

3. Load `.specify/templates/spec-template.md` to understand required sections.

4. Follow this execution flow:

    1. Parse user description from Input
       If empty: ERROR "No feature description provided"
    2. Extract key concepts from description
       Identify: actors, actions, data, constraints
    3. For unclear aspects:
       - Make informed guesses based on context and industry standards
       - Only mark with [NEEDS CLARIFICATION: specific question] if:
         - The choice significantly impacts feature scope or user experience
         - Multiple reasonable interpretations exist with different implications
         - No reasonable default exists
       - **LIMIT: Maximum 3 [NEEDS CLARIFICATION] markers total**
       - Prioritize clarifications by impact: scope > security/privacy > user experience > technical details
    4. Fill User Scenarios & Testing section
       If no clear user flow: ERROR "Cannot determine user scenarios"
    5. Generate Functional Requirements
       Each requirement must be testable
       Use reasonable defaults for unspecified details (document assumptions in Assumptions section)
    6. Define Success Criteria
       Create measurable, technology-agnostic outcomes
       Include both quantitative metrics (time, performance, volume) and qualitative measures (user satisfaction, task completion)
       Each criterion must be verifiable without implementation details
    7. Identify Key Entities (if data involved)
    8. Return: SUCCESS (spec ready for planning)

5. Write the specification to SPEC_FILE using the template structure, replacing placeholders with concrete details derived from the feature description (arguments) while preserving section order and headings.

6. **Specification Quality Validation**: After writing the initial spec, validate it against quality criteria:

   a. **Create Spec Quality Checklist**: Generate a checklist file at `FEATURE_DIR/checklists/requirements.md` using the checklist template structure with these validation items:

      ```markdown
      # Specification Quality Checklist: [FEATURE NAME]
      
      **Purpose**: Validate specification completeness and quality before proceeding to planning
      **Created**: [DATE]
      **Feature**: [Link to spec.md]
      
      ## Content Quality
      
      - [ ] No implementation details (languages, frameworks, APIs)
      - [ ] Focused on user value and business needs
      - [ ] Written for non-technical stakeholders
      - [ ] All mandatory sections completed
      
      ## Requirement Completeness
      
      - [ ] No [NEEDS CLARIFICATION] markers remain
      - [ ] Requirements are testable and unambiguous
      - [ ] Success criteria are measurable
      - [ ] Success criteria are technology-agnostic (no implementation details)
      - [ ] All acceptance scenarios are defined
      - [ ] Edge cases are identified
      - [ ] Scope is clearly bounded
      - [ ] Dependencies and assumptions identified
      
      ## Feature Readiness
      
      - [ ] All functional requirements have clear acceptance criteria
      - [ ] User scenarios cover primary flows
      - [ ] Feature meets measurable outcomes defined in Success Criteria
      - [ ] No implementation details leak into specification
      
      ## Notes
      
      - Items marked incomplete require spec updates before `/speckit.clarify` or `/speckit.plan`
      ```

   b. **Run Validation Check**: Review the spec against each checklist item:
      - For each item, determine if it passes or fails
      - Document specific issues found (quote relevant spec sections)

   c. **Handle Validation Results**:

      - **If all items pass**: Mark checklist complete and proceed to step 7

      - **If items fail (excluding [NEEDS CLARIFICATION])**:
        1. List the failing items and specific issues
        2. Update the spec to address each issue
        3. Re-run validation until all items pass (max 3 iterations)
        4. If still failing after 3 iterations, document remaining issues in checklist notes and warn user

      - **If [NEEDS CLARIFICATION] markers remain**:
        1. Extract all [NEEDS CLARIFICATION: ...] markers from the spec
        2. **LIMIT CHECK**: If more than 3 markers exist, keep only the 3 most critical (by scope/security/UX impact) and make informed guesses for the rest
        3. For each clarification needed (max 3), present options to user in this format:

           ```markdown
           ## Question [N]: [Topic]
           
           **Context**: [Quote relevant spec section]
           
           **What we need to know**: [Specific question from NEEDS CLARIFICATION marker]
           
           **Suggested Answers**:
           
           | Option | Answer | Implications |
           |--------|--------|--------------|
           | A      | [First suggested answer] | [What this means for the feature] |
           | B      | [Second suggested answer] | [What this means for the feature] |
           | C      | [Third suggested answer] | [What this means for the feature] |
           | Custom | Provide your own answer | [Explain how to provide custom input] |
           
           **Your choice**: _[Wait for user response]_
           ```

        4. **CRITICAL - Table Formatting**: Ensure markdown tables are properly formatted:
           - Use consistent spacing with pipes aligned
           - Each cell should have spaces around content: `| Content |` not `|Content|`
           - Header separator must have at least 3 dashes: `|--------|`
           - Test that the table renders correctly in markdown preview
        5. Number questions sequentially (Q1, Q2, Q3 - max 3 total)
        6. Present all questions together before waiting for responses
        7. Wait for user to respond with their choices for all questions (e.g., "Q1: A, Q2: Custom - [details], Q3: B")
        8. Update the spec by replacing each [NEEDS CLARIFICATION] marker with the user's selected or provided answer
        9. Re-run validation after all clarifications are resolved

   d. **Update Checklist**: After each validation iteration, update the checklist file with current pass/fail status

7. Report completion with branch name, spec file path, checklist results, and readiness for the next phase (`/speckit.clarify` or `/speckit.plan`).

8. **Check for extension hooks**: After reporting completion, check if `.specify/extensions.yml` exists in the project root.
   - If it exists, read it and look for entries under the `hooks.after_specify` key
   - If the YAML cannot be parsed or is invalid, skip hook checking silently and continue normally
   - Filter out hooks where `enabled` is explicitly `false`. Treat hooks without an `enabled` field as enabled by default.
   - For each remaining hook, do **not** attempt to interpret or evaluate hook `condition` expressions:
     - If the hook has no `condition` field, or it is null/empty, treat the hook as executable
     - If the hook defines a non-empty `condition`, skip the hook and leave condition evaluation to the HookExecutor implementation
   - For each executable hook, output the following based on its `optional` flag:
     - **Optional hook** (`optional: true`):
       ```
       ## Extension Hooks

       **Optional Hook**: {extension}
       Command: `/{command}`
       Description: {description}

       Prompt: {prompt}
       To execute: `/{command}`
       ```
     - **Mandatory hook** (`optional: false`):
       ```
       ## Extension Hooks

       **Automatic Hook**: {extension}
       Executing: `/{command}`
       EXECUTE_COMMAND: {command}
       ```
   - If no hooks are registered or `.specify/extensions.yml` does not exist, skip silently

**NOTE:** The script creates and checks out the new branch and initializes the spec file before writing.

## Quick Guidelines

- Focus on **WHAT** users need and **WHY**.
- Avoid HOW to implement (no tech stack, APIs, code structure).
- Written for business stakeholders, not developers.
- DO NOT create any checklists that are embedded in the spec. That will be a separate command.

### Section Requirements

- **Mandatory sections**: Must be completed for every feature
- **Optional sections**: Include only when relevant to the feature
- When a section doesn't apply, remove it entirely (don't leave as "N/A")

### For AI Generation

When creating this spec from a user prompt:

1. **Make informed guesses**: Use context, industry standards, and common patterns to fill gaps
2. **Document assumptions**: Record reasonable defaults in the Assumptions section
3. **Limit clarifications**: Maximum 3 [NEEDS CLARIFICATION] markers - use only for critical decisions that:
   - Significantly impact feature scope or user experience
   - Have multiple reasonable interpretations with different implications
   - Lack any reasonable default
4. **Prioritize clarifications**: scope > security/privacy > user experience > technical details
5. **Think like a tester**: Every vague requirement should fail the "testable and unambiguous" checklist item
6. **Common areas needing clarification** (only if no reasonable default exists):
   - Feature scope and boundaries (include/exclude specific use cases)
   - User types and permissions (if multiple conflicting interpretations possible)
   - Security/compliance requirements (when legally/financially significant)

**Examples of reasonable defaults** (don't ask about these):

- Data retention: Industry-standard practices for the domain
- Performance targets: Standard web/mobile app expectations unless specified
- Error handling: User-friendly messages with appropriate fallbacks
- Authentication method: Standard session-based or OAuth2 for web apps
- Integration patterns: Use project-appropriate patterns (REST/GraphQL for web services, function calls for libraries, CLI args for tools, etc.)

### Success Criteria Guidelines

Success criteria must be:

1. **Measurable**: Include specific metrics (time, percentage, count, rate)
2. **Technology-agnostic**: No mention of frameworks, languages, databases, or tools
3. **User-focused**: Describe outcomes from user/business perspective, not system internals
4. **Verifiable**: Can be tested/validated without knowing implementation details

**Good examples**:

- "Users can complete checkout in under 3 minutes"
- "System supports 10,000 concurrent users"
- "95% of searches return results in under 1 second"
- "Task completion rate improves by 40%"

**Bad examples** (implementation-focused):

- "API response time is under 200ms" (too technical, use "Users see results instantly")
- "Database can handle 1000 TPS" (implementation detail, use user-facing metric)
- "React components render efficiently" (framework-specific)
- "Redis cache hit rate above 80%" (technology-specific)

input.args: Define a specification-driven guild operations system that unifies economic planning and raid readiness validation by binding real-time market data to formal raid preparation contracts. The system ingests auction house data and character profiles (augmented with guild metadata), models consumables, materials, and role-specific requirements, and continuously computes both guild-level deficits and player-level readiness against a defined specification. It generates deterministic outputs including economic recommendations (BUY, FARM, WAIT) based on price trends and demand, and readiness classifications (READY, WARNING, NOT_READY) with explicit violations and remediation guidance. The novel step is the integration of a spec-native control plane that treats both resource acquisition (gold/materials) and player preparation as machine-checkable invariants, enabling continuous validation and decision-making across the full raid value stream. By linking market conditions directly to readiness requirements, the system shifts guild coordination from reactive and manual processes to a unified, automated, and outcome-aligned workflow, with optional drift detection to identify when economic state or player readiness falls out of compliance prior to raid execution.

# Feature Specification: Guild Operations System

**Feature Branch**: `001-define-specification-driven`  
**Created**: 2026-04-08  
**Status**: Draft  
**Input**: User description: "Define a specification-driven guild operations system that unifies economic planning and raid readiness validation by binding real-time market data to formal raid preparation contracts."

## User Scenarios & Testing *(mandatory)*

### User Story 1 - Guild-Level Economic Dashboard (Priority: P1)

A guild leader opens the operations dashboard before raid night to understand the guild's overall economic position. The system has already ingested the latest auction house snapshot and computed material deficits against the upcoming raid's consumable requirements. The leader sees a summary of guild-wide shortfalls (e.g., "23 Elixirs of Shadow Power needed, 8 on hand across guild bank and confirmed player inventories"), with each deficit item tagged with an economic recommendation: BUY (market price is below average and trending down), FARM (price is above threshold, materials are farmable), or WAIT (price is spiking and expected to normalize). The leader uses this to issue coordinated directives rather than relying on individual players to figure it out.

**Why this priority**: Without a guild-level economic picture, all downstream readiness checks lack context. This is the foundational data layer that every other story depends on for value.

**Independent Test**: Can be fully tested by loading a static auction house snapshot and a raid consumable requirement list, then verifying the system produces correct deficit counts and economic recommendations for each item.

**Acceptance Scenarios**:

1. **Given** an auction house snapshot and a raid specification requiring 25 Greater Fire Protection Potions, **When** the guild bank holds 10 and confirmed player inventories hold 5, **Then** the system reports a deficit of 10 and assigns an economic recommendation based on current price trend.
2. **Given** a consumable whose auction house price is 30% below its 7-day moving average and trending downward, **When** the deficit computation runs, **Then** the system assigns a BUY recommendation for that consumable.
3. **Given** a consumable whose auction house price is 50% above its 7-day moving average, **When** the deficit computation runs, **Then** the system assigns a FARM recommendation if the material is farmable, or WAIT if it is not.
4. **Given** no auction house data is available for a specific consumable, **When** the deficit computation runs, **Then** the system flags that item as "NO MARKET DATA" and defaults to a FARM recommendation.

---

### User Story 2 - Player Readiness Validation (Priority: P1)

A raid leader reviews the readiness status of each confirmed raider ahead of a scheduled raid. The system evaluates each player's character profile against the raid preparation contract (a formal specification of what each role requires: consumables, enchantments, gear minimums, talent builds). Each player is classified as READY, WARNING, or NOT_READY with an explicit list of violations (e.g., "Missing: Flask of Supreme Power, Enchant Weapon: Spell Power") and remediation guidance (e.g., "Available on AH for 45g — recommend BUY" or "Craftable by Alchemist Toadsworth in guild").

**Why this priority**: Player readiness validation is the core differentiator — moving from "did everyone prepare?" as a trust-based question to a machine-checkable invariant. Tied with P1 because it directly consumes the economic data from Story 1.

**Independent Test**: Can be fully tested by loading a character profile, a role-specific requirement contract, and verifying the system produces the correct classification with violations and remediation text.

**Acceptance Scenarios**:

1. **Given** a Mage character profile with all required consumables, enchantments, and gear meeting the raid contract, **When** readiness validation runs, **Then** the player is classified as READY with zero violations.
2. **Given** a Warrior tank character profile missing a required flask and one enchantment, **When** readiness validation runs, **Then** the player is classified as NOT_READY with two violations listing the missing flask and enchantment, each with remediation guidance.
3. **Given** a Healer character profile that meets all mandatory requirements but is missing an optional consumable, **When** readiness validation runs, **Then** the player is classified as WARNING with one advisory violation.
4. **Given** a player whose gear score falls below the raid contract minimum, **When** readiness validation runs, **Then** the system lists each gear slot that is below threshold with the current item and the minimum expected.

---

### User Story 3 - Raid Preparation Contract Definition (Priority: P2)

A guild officer defines or updates a raid preparation contract for an upcoming raid (e.g., "Karazhan — Tuesday 8pm"). The contract specifies, per role (tank, healer, melee DPS, ranged DPS), the required consumables, enchantments, minimum gear expectations, and talent build guidelines. The contract also specifies guild-level material requirements (e.g., reagents for group buffs, repair bot materials). Once saved, this contract becomes the specification against which all readiness checks and economic computations are evaluated.

**Why this priority**: Contracts are the specification that drives Stories 1 and 2. However, the system can ship with a default/sample contract and still deliver value, so this is P2 — important but not the first thing to prove.

**Independent Test**: Can be fully tested by creating a contract, then running readiness validation and deficit computation against it to verify outputs change correctly when the contract changes.

**Acceptance Scenarios**:

1. **Given** an officer creates a new raid contract specifying that all tanks require Ironshield Potion, **When** the contract is saved, **Then** subsequent readiness checks for tank-role players validate against this requirement.
2. **Given** an existing contract is updated to remove a previously required consumable, **When** a player who was previously NOT_READY due to that consumable is re-evaluated, **Then** that violation no longer appears.
3. **Given** a contract specifies role-specific requirements, **When** a player's assigned role changes from DPS to Healer, **Then** the player is validated against the Healer requirements, not the DPS requirements.

---

### User Story 4 - Drift Detection and Compliance Alerts (Priority: P3)

Between raid scheduling and raid execution, the system continuously monitors for drift — changes in economic conditions or player readiness that move the guild out of compliance. If auction house prices spike for a critical consumable after the guild committed to a BUY strategy, or if a player who was READY becomes NOT_READY (e.g., they respecced or sold required items), the system generates a drift alert with the nature of the change, the impact on overall readiness, and recommended corrective action.

**Why this priority**: Drift detection is a high-value differentiator but requires Stories 1-3 to be functional first. It transforms the system from a point-in-time checker to a continuous compliance monitor.

**Independent Test**: Can be fully tested by running an initial readiness evaluation, then modifying economic data or player profile data and verifying the system detects and reports the change.

**Acceptance Scenarios**:

1. **Given** a guild is at 100% readiness at time T1, **When** auction house prices for a required consumable increase by 40% at time T2, **Then** the system generates a drift alert reclassifying the economic recommendation from BUY to WAIT and noting the cost impact.
2. **Given** a player was READY at time T1, **When** their character profile updates at T2 showing a removed enchantment, **Then** the system generates a drift alert with the player's new classification and the specific violation.
3. **Given** drift detection is configured as optional, **When** a guild officer disables drift detection for a specific raid, **Then** no drift alerts are generated for that raid.

---

### User Story 5 - Guild Readiness Summary Report (Priority: P3)

Before raid time, a guild leader generates a summary report showing overall guild readiness: percentage of raiders READY vs WARNING vs NOT_READY, total estimated gold cost to close all deficits at current market prices, a prioritized list of actions (e.g., "3 players need flasks — guild bank has 2, buy 1 at 38g"), and a go/no-go recommendation based on configurable thresholds (e.g., "GO if >= 80% READY").

**Why this priority**: This is the synthesis layer that turns raw data into an actionable decision. It depends on all upstream stories functioning correctly.

**Independent Test**: Can be fully tested by loading pre-computed readiness and deficit data and verifying the summary output matches expected values.

**Acceptance Scenarios**:

1. **Given** 20 raiders where 16 are READY, 3 are WARNING, and 1 is NOT_READY, **When** the summary report generates with a go-threshold of 80%, **Then** the report outputs "GO" (80% READY meets threshold) with a breakdown of all player statuses.
2. **Given** total guild deficits of 5 consumable types, **When** the summary report generates, **Then** each deficit line shows current market cost, recommended action, and whether guild bank stock can partially fill it.

---

### Edge Cases

- What happens when the auction house API returns stale data older than a configurable threshold? The system MUST flag the data age and optionally degrade recommendations to conservative defaults (FARM for all unknowns).
- How does the system handle a player who is in the raid roster but has no character profile data available? The system MUST classify them as NOT_READY with a "NO PROFILE DATA" violation.
- What happens when a raid contract references a consumable that does not exist in the item database? The system MUST reject the contract with a validation error identifying the unknown item.
- How does the system handle duplicate players (same character, multiple roster entries)? The system MUST deduplicate by character identity and use the most recent profile data.
- What happens when market data shows zero listings for a required consumable? The system MUST flag it as "UNAVAILABLE ON MARKET" and recommend FARM if the item is craftable/farmable, or escalate to guild leadership if not.

## Requirements *(mandatory)*

### Functional Requirements

- **FR-001**: System MUST ingest auction house data snapshots including item listings, prices, quantities, and historical price trends.
- **FR-002**: System MUST ingest character profile data including equipped gear, inventory, enchantments, talent build, and assigned raid role.
- **FR-003**: System MUST support guild metadata augmentation on character profiles, including guild bank contents, guild rank, and officer-assigned notes.
- **FR-004**: System MUST model consumables, raw materials, and crafted items with relationships between them (e.g., a flask requires specific herbs and a vial).
- **FR-005**: System MUST support the definition of raid preparation contracts specifying per-role requirements for consumables, enchantments, minimum gear expectations, and talent guidelines.
- **FR-006**: System MUST validate raid preparation contracts against the known item/consumable database and reject contracts referencing unknown items.
- **FR-007**: System MUST compute guild-level deficits by comparing total requirements (across all rostered players) against available supply (guild bank + confirmed player inventories).
- **FR-008**: System MUST generate deterministic economic recommendations (BUY, FARM, WAIT) for each deficit item based on current price, historical price trend, and configurable thresholds.
- **FR-009**: System MUST evaluate each rostered player's profile against the raid preparation contract and classify them as READY, WARNING, or NOT_READY.
- **FR-010**: System MUST produce an explicit list of violations for any player classified as WARNING or NOT_READY, with each violation describing what is missing and how to remediate it.
- **FR-011**: System MUST link economic data to remediation guidance, so that violation remediation includes current market availability and cost information.
- **FR-012**: System MUST generate a guild-level readiness summary including player status distribution, total deficit cost estimate, and a configurable go/no-go threshold.
- **FR-013**: System MUST support optional drift detection that compares current state against a previous baseline and reports changes in economic conditions or player readiness.
- **FR-014**: System MUST produce all outputs deterministically — the same inputs MUST always produce the same outputs, with no randomness or non-deterministic ordering.
- **FR-015**: System MUST allow guild officers to configure economic thresholds (e.g., what percentage below average triggers BUY, what percentage above triggers FARM/WAIT).
- **FR-016**: System MUST support multiple concurrent raid contracts (e.g., Karazhan Tuesday and Gruul's Lair Thursday) with independent readiness tracking.
- **FR-017**: System MUST handle missing or incomplete data gracefully by flagging gaps rather than silently skipping them.

### Key Entities

- **Raid Preparation Contract**: A formal specification for a specific raid event defining role-based requirements (consumables, enchantments, gear minimums, talent guidelines) and guild-level material needs. Linked to a raid instance, date/time, and roster.
- **Character Profile**: A player's character state including equipped gear, inventory, enchantments, talent build, profession skills, and assigned raid role. Augmented with guild-specific metadata (guild bank access, officer notes).
- **Auction House Snapshot**: A point-in-time capture of market data including item listings, unit prices, available quantities, and derived metrics (moving averages, trend direction).
- **Item/Consumable Model**: A catalog entry for a game item including its type (consumable, material, gear), crafting recipe relationships, farmability flag, and known sources.
- **Economic Recommendation**: A computed directive (BUY, FARM, WAIT) for a specific item deficit, derived from price trends, configurable thresholds, and item properties.
- **Readiness Classification**: A computed status (READY, WARNING, NOT_READY) for a player or guild, with associated violation details and remediation guidance.
- **Drift Event**: A detected change between two evaluation points in either economic conditions or player readiness, with impact assessment and corrective action recommendation.
- **Guild Roster**: The list of confirmed players for a specific raid event, each with an assigned role and linked character profile.

## Success Criteria *(mandatory)*

### Measurable Outcomes

- **SC-001**: Guild leaders can view a complete economic deficit report for an upcoming raid within 30 seconds of requesting it.
- **SC-002**: The system correctly classifies 100% of rostered players against a raid preparation contract, with zero false READY classifications (a player missing a requirement is never shown as READY).
- **SC-003**: Economic recommendations align with observable market conditions — when an item's price is below the configured threshold, BUY is recommended; above threshold, FARM or WAIT is recommended, verifiable against historical snapshots.
- **SC-004**: Drift detection identifies 100% of readiness state changes (player or economic) between evaluation points, with alerts generated within one evaluation cycle.
- **SC-005**: Guild officers can define or update a raid preparation contract in under 5 minutes.
- **SC-006**: The readiness summary report reduces pre-raid coordination overhead by eliminating manual inventory checks — officers no longer need to whisper individual players to confirm preparation.
- **SC-007**: All system outputs are deterministic — running the same evaluation twice with identical inputs produces byte-identical results.
- **SC-008**: The system handles a guild roster of up to 40 players with up to 15 distinct consumable/enchantment requirements per role without degradation in response time.

## Assumptions

- The system targets World of Warcraft: The Burning Crusade (TBC) Classic, with item databases, consumable lists, and raid content specific to that expansion.
- Auction house data is available via an external data source that provides periodic snapshots; the system does not scrape or interact with the game client directly.
- Character profile data is provided via an external source (e.g., a game data addon export or third-party API); the system does not read game memory.
- Guild bank contents are included in the character profile data source or provided separately by an officer.
- A single guild is the unit of operation; multi-guild support is out of scope for v1.
- The system operates as an out-of-game tool; it does not integrate with in-game UI or addons.
- Users (guild leaders, officers, players) access the system via a local application or service; web/mobile deployment is out of scope for v1.
- Price trend calculations use a simple moving average over a configurable lookback window; advanced statistical models are out of scope for v1.
- The item/consumable database is pre-populated with TBC Classic items; the system does not need to discover items dynamically.

# Feature Specification: Guild Operations System

**Feature Branch**: `001-define-specification-driven`  
**Created**: 2026-04-08  
**Status**: Draft  
**Input**: User description: "Define a specification-driven guild operations system that unifies economic planning and raid readiness validation by binding real-time market data to formal raid preparation contracts."

## User Scenarios & Testing *(mandatory)*

### User Story 1 - Guild-Level Economic Dashboard (Priority: P1)

A guild leader opens the operations dashboard before raid night to understand the guild's overall economic position. The system has already ingested the latest auction house snapshot and computed material deficits against the upcoming raid's consumable requirements. The leader uses this to issue coordinated directives rather than relying on individual players to figure it out.

**Why this priority**: Without a guild-level economic picture, all downstream readiness checks lack context. This is the foundational data layer that every other story depends on for value.

**Independent Test**: Can be fully tested by loading a static auction house snapshot and a raid consumable requirement list, then verifying the system produces correct deficit counts and economic recommendations for each item.

**Acceptance Scenarios**:

2. **Given** a consumable whose auction house price is 30% below its 7-day moving average and trending downward, **When** the deficit computation runs, **Then** the system assigns a BUY recommendation for that consumable.
3. **Given** a consumable whose auction house price is 50% above its 7-day moving average, **When** the deficit computation runs, **Then** the system assigns a FARM recommendation if the material is farmable, or WAIT if it is not.

+2. **Given** a consumable whose auction house price is 30% below its 7-day moving average and trending downward, **When** the deficit computation runs, **Then** the system assigns a BUY recommendation for that consumable.

+3. **Given** a consumable whose auction house price is 50% above its 7-day moving average, **When** the deficit computation runs, **Then** the system assigns a FARM recommendation if the material is farmable, or WAIT if it is not.

+4. **Given** no auction house data is available for a specific consumable, **When** the deficit computation runs, **Then** the system flags that item as "NO MARKET DATA" and defaults to a FARM recommendation.
+### User Story 2 - Player Readiness Validation (Priority: P1)

+A raid leader reviews the readiness status of each confirmed raider ahead of a scheduled raid. The system evaluates each player's character profile against the raid preparation contract (a formal specification of what each role requires: consumables, enchantments, gear minimums, talent builds). Each player is classified as READY, WARNING, or NOT_READY with an explicit list of violations (e.g., "Missing: Flask of Supreme Power, Enchant Weapon: Spell Power") and remediation guidance (e.g., "Available on AH for 45g — recommend BUY" or "Craftable by Alchemist Toadsworth in guild").

+**Why this priority**: Player readiness validation is the core differentiator — moving from "did everyone prepare?" as a trust-based question to a machine-checkable invariant. Tied with P1 because it directly consumes the economic data from Story 1.

+**Independent Test**: Can be fully tested by loading a character profile, a role-specific requirement contract, and verifying the system produces the correct classification with violations and remediation text.

+1. **Given** a Mage character profile with all required consumables, enchantments, and gear meeting the raid contract, **When** readiness validation runs, **Then** the player is classified as READY with zero violations.

+2. **Given** a Warrior tank character profile missing a required flask and one enchantment, **When** readiness validation runs, **Then** the player is classified as NOT_READY with two violations listing the missing flask and enchantment, each with remediation guidance.

+3. **Given** a Healer character profile that meets all mandatory requirements but is missing an optional consumable, **When** readiness validation runs, **Then** the player is classified as WARNING with one advisory violation.

+4. **Given** a player whose gear score falls below the raid contract minimum, **When** readiness validation runs, **Then** the system lists each gear slot that is below threshold with the current item and the minimum expected.
+### User Story 3 - Raid Preparation Contract Definition (Priority: P2)
-[Describe this user journey in plain language]

+A guild officer defines or updates a raid preparation contract for an upcoming raid (e.g., "Karazhan — Tuesday 8pm"). The contract specifies, per role (tank, healer, melee DPS, ranged DPS), the required consumables, enchantments, minimum gear expectations, and talent build guidelines. The contract also specifies guild-level material requirements (e.g., reagents for group buffs, repair bot materials). Once saved, this contract becomes the specification against which all readiness checks and economic computations are evaluated.
-**Why this priority**: [Explain the value and why it has this priority level]

+**Why this priority**: Contracts are the specification that drives Stories 1 and 2. However, the system can ship with a default/sample contract and still deliver value, so this is P2 — important but not the first thing to prove.
-**Independent Test**: [Describe how this can be tested independently]
+**Independent Test**: Can be fully tested by creating a contract, then running readiness validation and deficit computation against it to verify outputs change correctly when the contract changes.

+1. **Given** an officer creates a new raid contract specifying that all tanks require Ironshield Potion, **When** the contract is saved, **Then** subsequent readiness checks for tank-role players validate against this requirement.

+2. **Given** an existing contract is updated to remove a previously required consumable, **When** a player who was previously NOT_READY due to that consumable is re-evaluated, **Then** that violation no longer appears.

+3. **Given** a contract specifies role-specific requirements, **When** a player's assigned role changes from DPS to Healer, **Then** the player is validated against the Healer requirements, not the DPS requirements.
+### User Story 4 - Drift Detection and Compliance Alerts (Priority: P3)
-[Describe this user journey in plain language]

+Between raid scheduling and raid execution, the system continuously monitors for drift — changes in economic conditions or player readiness that move the guild out of compliance. If auction house prices spike for a critical consumable after the guild committed to a BUY strategy, or if a player who was READY becomes NOT_READY (e.g., they respecced or sold required items), the system generates a drift alert with the nature of the change, the impact on overall readiness, and recommended corrective action.
-**Why this priority**: [Explain the value and why it has this priority level]

+**Why this priority**: Drift detection is a high-value differentiator but requires Stories 1-3 to be functional first. It transforms the system from a point-in-time checker to a continuous compliance monitor.
-**Independent Test**: [Describe how this can be tested independently]
+**Independent Test**: Can be fully tested by running an initial readiness evaluation, then modifying economic data or player profile data and verifying the system detects and reports the change.

+1. **Given** a guild is at 100% readiness at time T1, **When** auction house prices for a required consumable increase by 40% at time T2, **Then** the system generates a drift alert reclassifying the economic recommendation from BUY to WAIT and noting the cost impact.

+2. **Given** a player was READY at time T1, **When** their character profile updates at T2 showing a removed enchantment, **Then** the system generates a drift alert with the player's new classification and the specific violation.
+3. **Given** drift detection is configured as optional, **When** a guild officer disables drift detection for a specific raid, **Then** no drift alerts are generated for that raid.
-[Add more user stories as needed, each with an assigned priority]
+### User Story 5 - Guild Readiness Summary Report (Priority: P3)

+Before raid time, a guild leader generates a summary report showing overall guild readiness: percentage of raiders READY vs WARNING vs NOT_READY, total estimated gold cost to close all deficits at current market prices, a prioritized list of actions (e.g., "3 players need flasks — guild bank has 2, buy 1 at 38g"), and a go/no-go recommendation based on configurable thresholds (e.g., "GO if >= 80% READY").
-  ACTION REQUIRED: The content in this section represents placeholders.
-  Fill them out with the right edge cases.
+**Why this priority**: This is the synthesis layer that turns raw data into an actionable decision. It depends on all upstream stories functioning correctly.
+**Independent Test**: Can be fully tested by loading pre-computed readiness and deficit data and verifying the summary output matches expected values.
-  ACTION REQUIRED: The content in this section represents placeholders.
-  Fill them out with the right functional requirements.

+1. **Given** 20 raiders where 16 are READY, 3 are WARNING, and 1 is NOT_READY, **When** the summary report generates with a go-threshold of 80%, **Then** the report outputs "GO" (80% READY meets threshold) with a breakdown of all player statuses.

+2. **Given** total guild deficits of 5 consumable types, **When** the summary report generates, **Then** each deficit line shows current market cost, recommended action, and whether guild bank stock can partially fill it.

-- **FR-002**: System MUST [specific capability, e.g., "validate email addresses"]
-- **FR-003**: Users MUST be able to [key interaction, e.g., "reset their password"]

+- What happens when the auction house API returns stale data older than a configurable threshold? The system MUST flag the data age and optionally degrade recommendations to conservative defaults (FARM for all unknowns).
+- How does the system handle a player who is in the raid roster but has no character profile data available? The system MUST classify them as NOT_READY with a "NO PROFILE DATA" violation.
+- What happens when a raid contract references a consumable that does not exist in the item database? The system MUST reject the contract with a validation error identifying the unknown item.
+- How does the system handle duplicate players (same character, multiple roster entries)? The system MUST deduplicate by character identity and use the most recent profile data.

+- What happens when market data shows zero listings for a required consumable? The system MUST flag it as "UNAVAILABLE ON MARKET" and recommend FARM if the item is craftable/farmable, or escalate to guild leadership if not.
-- **FR-006**: System MUST authenticate users via [NEEDS CLARIFICATION: auth method not specified - email/password, SSO, OAuth?]
-- **FR-007**: System MUST retain user data for [NEEDS CLARIFICATION: retention period not specified]
-### Key Entities *(include if feature involves data)*
-- **[Entity 1]**: [What it represents, key attributes without implementation]
-- **[Entity 2]**: [What it represents, relationships to other entities]
+- **FR-001**: System MUST ingest auction house data snapshots including item listings, prices, quantities, and historical price trends.
+- **FR-002**: System MUST ingest character profile data including equipped gear, inventory, enchantments, talent build, and assigned raid role.
+- **FR-003**: System MUST support guild metadata augmentation on character profiles, including guild bank contents, guild rank, and officer-assigned notes.
+- **FR-004**: System MUST model consumables, raw materials, and crafted items with relationships between them (e.g., a flask requires specific herbs and a vial).
+- **FR-005**: System MUST support the definition of raid preparation contracts specifying per-role requirements for consumables, enchantments, minimum gear expectations, and talent guidelines.
+- **FR-006**: System MUST validate raid preparation contracts against the known item/consumable database and reject contracts referencing unknown items.
+- **FR-007**: System MUST compute guild-level deficits by comparing total requirements (across all rostered players) against available supply (guild bank + confirmed player inventories).
+- **FR-008**: System MUST generate deterministic economic recommendations (BUY, FARM, WAIT) for each deficit item based on current price, historical price trend, and configurable thresholds.
+- **FR-009**: System MUST evaluate each rostered player's profile against the raid preparation contract and classify them as READY, WARNING, or NOT_READY.
+- **FR-010**: System MUST produce an explicit list of violations for any player classified as WARNING or NOT_READY, with each violation describing what is missing and how to remediate it.
+- **FR-011**: System MUST link economic data to remediation guidance, so that violation remediation includes current market availability and cost information.
+- **FR-012**: System MUST generate a guild-level readiness summary including player status distribution, total deficit cost estimate, and a configurable go/no-go threshold.
+- **FR-013**: System MUST support optional drift detection that compares current state against a previous baseline and reports changes in economic conditions or player readiness.
+- **FR-014**: System MUST produce all outputs deterministically — the same inputs MUST always produce the same outputs, with no randomness or non-deterministic ordering.
+- **FR-015**: System MUST allow guild officers to configure economic thresholds (e.g., what percentage below average triggers BUY, what percentage above triggers FARM/WAIT).
+- **FR-016**: System MUST support multiple concurrent raid contracts (e.g., Karazhan Tuesday and Gruul's Lair Thursday) with independent readiness tracking.
+- **FR-017**: System MUST handle missing or incomplete data gracefully by flagging gaps rather than silently skipping them.
-  ACTION REQUIRED: Define measurable success criteria.
-  These must be technology-agnostic and measurable.

+- **Raid Preparation Contract**: A formal specification for a specific raid event defining role-based requirements (consumables, enchantments, gear minimums, talent guidelines) and guild-level material needs. Linked to a raid instance, date/time, and roster.

+- **Character Profile**: A player's character state including equipped gear, inventory, enchantments, talent build, profession skills, and assigned raid role. Augmented with guild-specific metadata (guild bank access, officer notes).
+- **Auction House Snapshot**: A point-in-time capture of market data including item listings, unit prices, available quantities, and derived metrics (moving averages, trend direction).
+- **Item/Consumable Model**: A catalog entry for a game item including its type (consumable, material, gear), crafting recipe relationships, farmability flag, and known sources.
+- **Economic Recommendation**: A computed directive (BUY, FARM, WAIT) for a specific item deficit, derived from price trends, configurable thresholds, and item properties.
+- **Readiness Classification**: A computed status (READY, WARNING, NOT_READY) for a player or guild, with associated violation details and remediation guidance.
+- **Drift Event**: A detected change between two evaluation points in either economic conditions or player readiness, with impact assessment and corrective action recommendation.
+- **Guild Roster**: The list of confirmed players for a specific raid event, each with an assigned role and linked character profile.
-- **SC-001**: [Measurable metric, e.g., "Users can complete account creation in under 2 minutes"]
-- **SC-002**: [Measurable metric, e.g., "System handles 1000 concurrent users without degradation"]
-- **SC-003**: [User satisfaction metric, e.g., "90% of users successfully complete primary task on first attempt"]
-- **SC-004**: [Business metric, e.g., "Reduce support tickets related to [X] by 50%"]
+- **SC-001**: Guild leaders can view a complete economic deficit report for an upcoming raid within 30 seconds of requesting it.

+- **SC-002**: The system correctly classifies 100% of rostered players against a raid preparation contract, with zero false READY classifications (a player missing a requirement is never shown as READY).

+- **SC-003**: Economic recommendations align with observable market conditions — when an item's price is below the configured threshold, BUY is recommended; above threshold, FARM or WAIT is recommended, verifiable against historical snapshots.
+- **SC-004**: Drift detection identifies 100% of readiness state changes (player or economic) between evaluation points, with alerts generated within one evaluation cycle.
+- **SC-005**: Guild officers can define or update a raid preparation contract in under 5 minutes.

+- **SC-006**: The readiness summary report reduces pre-raid coordination overhead by eliminating manual inventory checks — officers no longer need to whisper individual players to confirm preparation.
+- **SC-007**: All system outputs are deterministic — running the same evaluation twice with identical inputs produces byte-identical results.
+- **SC-008**: The system handles a guild roster of up to 40 players with up to 15 distinct consumable/enchantment requirements per role without degradation in response time.
-  ACTION REQUIRED: The content in this section represents placeholders.
-  Fill them out with the right assumptions based on reasonable defaults
-  chosen when the feature description did not specify certain details.
-- [Assumption about target users, e.g., "Users have stable internet connectivity"]
-- [Assumption about scope boundaries, e.g., "Mobile support is out of scope for v1"]
-- [Assumption about data/environment, e.g., "Existing authentication system will be reused"]
-- [Dependency on existing system/service, e.g., "Requires access to the existing user profile API"]
+- The system targets World of Warcraft: The Burning Crusade (TBC) Classic, with item databases, consumable lists, and raid content specific to that expansion.
+- Auction house data is available via an external data source that provides periodic snapshots; the system does not scrape or interact with the game client directly.
+- Character profile data is provided via an external source (e.g., a game data addon export or third-party API); the system does not read game memory.
+- Guild bank contents are included in the character profile data source or provided separately by an officer.
+- A single guild is the unit of operation; multi-guild support is out of scope for v1.
+- The system operates as an out-of-game tool; it does not integrate with in-game UI or addons.
+- Users (guild leaders, officers, players) access the system via a local application or service; web/mobile deployment is out of scope for v1.
+- Price trend calculations use a simple moving average over a configurable lookback window; advanced statistical models are out of scope for v1.
+- The item/consumable database is pre-populated with TBC Classic items; the system does not need to discover items dynamically.
